proof_stages.label: Random Mantel formulation
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces G(n,p), defines T_3(G) as the largest triangle-free subgraph size and B_3(G) as the largest bipartite subgraph/max-cut size, recalls Mantel's theorem, and asks when asymptotically almost surely T_3(G)=B_3(G).

proof_stages.label: Threshold scale, prior work, and main theorem
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing the constant-p and n^{-c} results of Simonovits--Sós/Szemerédi and Brightwell--Łuczak--Stenger, the speaker explains that the relevant scale is where every edge has common neighbors and states the DeMarco--Kahn theorem for p at least C n^{-1/2} sqrt(log n), while emphasizing that the property is not an increasing threshold property.

proof_stages.label: Sparse Turán and stability framework
proof_stages.summary: The problem is placed in the broader K_r-free setting via T_r(G) and B_r(G), the conjectural p-scale for equality is stated, and the sparse Turán-density and stability theorems of Conlon--Gowers, Schacht, and Kohayakawa--Łuczak--Rödl are identified, especially the r=3 stability theorem used as structural input.

proof_stages.label: Parity and clique-complex homology detour
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by avoiding sparse regularity, the speaker isolates the exact condition that a subgraph meet every triangle evenly, states that at the main p-scale such a subgraph must be a cut, reformulates this as vanishing of H_1 of the clique complex over Z_2, and proposes the higher-dimensional homology analogue.

proof_stages.label: Local max-cut obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The proof idea begins by reducing Mantel equality to ruling out max cuts A,B with an internal edge xy in A having no common neighbors in B, since then adding xy to the cut would create a larger triangle-free subgraph.

proof_stages.label: Delayed exposure and constrained-cut estimate
proof_stages.summary: The graph is exposed in stages, postponing the edges from y to N(x), and a constrained maximum cut of the partial graph is used to show with very high probability that every max cut placing x and y together has about n p^2/2 common neighbors for xy across the cut.

proof_stages.label: Triangle-cost bookkeeping and stability completion
proof_stages.summary: The final mechanism is that each edge placed inside a part of a putative maximum triangle-free subgraph creates many triangles and forces the loss of many cut edges, and with the stability theorem and a best-fitting cut this bookkeeping yields that no triangle-free subgraph beats the maximum cut.
